name: Aesthetics of Power
description:
Horticultural Building, Lal Bagh, Wiele Studio, Late 19th-Early 20th century, Albumen print, PHY.21244.8.1
Join us at the exhibition Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire, and unravel the entangled histories of colonial-era classification of the natural world through botanical illustration. Drawing on select illustrations from the exhibition, Abhishek Khan discusses the roles and aesthetics of gardens and garden-making, and their colonial preference over native sacred groves.  He goes on to examine how these images emerged from masculine frameworks and compares them to native botanical art and contemporary works by women artists.

name: Nocturnes
description:
Image credits: Metamorphosis Film Junction
An immersive viewing experience of sound and imagery, the film weaves together an intricate and poetic tapestry of our world. In the dense forests of Arunachal Pradesh, ecologist Mansi sets out on a quest to study moths in one of the most vibrant places on earth. She teams up with Bicki, a young man from the indigenous Bugun community, to seek clues about what the future holds for the moths as temperatures rise. 

By focusing on a small, ephemeral, nocturnal creature like the moth, Nocturnes seeks to question a human-centric view of the world. The lush forest, throbbing with a vast diversity of life, emerges as a breath-taking character as the film responds to the symphony of sounds and the inherent rhythms of the trees, the wind and the rain. The result is a rare and transformative experience that invites us all to look with more attention and care at the hidden interconnections in nature. 

This event is held in conjunction with our ongoing permanent exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky.

name: Holding Space: Feminist Reading Circle
description:
What does vulnerability look like? What does care feel like?
These are the questions that artist afra eisma leaves us with in warrior garments.

Join us for the final session of reflective feminist reading sessions, developed in collaboration with Zubaan and facilitated by Laxmi Murthy. Together, we will explore gender-based violence—its lived realities, and the possibilities of resistance, solidarity, and healing. Through stories, essays, and personal reflections, you are invited to engage with the exhibition at your own pace, in a space that encourages listening, sharing, and care.

Note: This is the final session in a series presented alongside Warrior Garments. Reading materials for each will be shared after registration.

Trigger Warning: The sessions may include discussions on sexual violence.

name: A Fragrance Walk at Lalbagh Botanical Garden
description:
Magnolia campbellii H.f. & T; Illustrations of Himalayan Plants; Author & Artist: Joseph Dalton Hooker; Lithographer: Walter Hood Fitch; 1855; Image courtesy of the Missouri Botanical Garden Library via the Biodiversity Heritage Library
In this walk, Abhishek Khan traces the backstory of the few surviving—and many lost—flowers and plants that were painted in Lalbagh. As we observe the fragrant flowers and leaves around us, we learn how today's botanical collection are a product of the aesthetics established by colonial empire, continuing to influence what we see in gardens today. 

This programme is in conjunction with MAP’s ongoing exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire.

name: Prints in Blue
description:
Untitled, Durgaprasad Bandi,2022, Cyanotype, MAC.02155
This workshop introduces participants to cyanotype process through direct engagement with materials, light, and composition. Rooted in the legacy of early photographic image-making, the session moves from technical understanding to exploratory making.

Beginning with an introduction to the history and context of cyanotype, participants then prepare coated surfaces, compose images using botanical specimens, and expose prints using sunlight and UV sources. 

On experimentation with form, layering, and variations in exposure and washing, participants understand how the process responds to material and environmental conditions. 

This programme is in conjunction with MAP’s ongoing exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire.

name: Between Seeing and Listening
description:
Image credits: Metamorphosis Film Junction
The workshop evolves from the core ideas of the film Nocturnes. We will together experience, discuss, and go deeper into the interface between duration and attention through a range of guided individual and group exercises: listening to field recordings, viewing film excerpts, taking photographs and videos, and recording sounds. You will have an opportunity to explore and interpret the world around you through images, sounds, and unexpected ways in which they interact and speak to each other. No prior technical knowledge is required. All we ask is for you to be present with an open and curious mind.

Attending the screening of Nocturnes on 19 June is highly recommended. 

This event is held in conjunction with our ongoing permanent exhibition Beneath the Turning Sky.
